request.question: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?

title: Pippa Passes, Kentucky
Saluta Rocks
10 Downing Street
Uriconian
On the Rocks (play)
Snag Rocks
Ramp Rocks
Recreation room
Battle of Muhu
Drawing room play
sentences: Pippa Passes is a home rule-class city located along Caney Creek in Knott County, eastern Kentucky, United States.
Its formal name was chosen to honor benefactors of Alice Lloyd College.
After more than a century, its residents commonly call the community as Caney or Caney Creek.
The population was 533 at the time of the year 2010 U.S. Census.
The small city is located in the mountainous Appalachia region, an area of coal mining.
Saluta Rocks ( ) is a group of rocks 1 nautical mile (1.9 km) east of Laurie Point, lying off the south coast and near the west end of South Georgia.
The name Mutt and Jeff was probably given by Lieutenant Commander J.M. Chaplin of the 1926.
The SGS, 1955–56, reported that the name is misleading; there are not two rocks as implied, but a group.
The rocks were renamed by the United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) for the Saluta, a transport of the South Georgia Whaling Co.
for many years.

10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.
The Uriconian rocks of Shropshire (Wrekin Terrane) are thought to be potentially related to the Longmyndian Supergroup of the Stretton Hills, Shropshire, United Kingdom.
Current geological profiling of the terranes suggests that the Uriconian rocks are of Precambrian age (Neoproterozoic Phases 2 and 3).
The Uriconian Rocks outcrop to the southeast of the Long Mynd area of the Welsh Borderland Fault System and beyond the Church Stretton Fault which trends northeast-southwest across the area.
The Stretton Hills are composed primarily of arenaceous (quartz rich sand) beds assigned to the Longmyndian Supergroup.
The Longmyndian rocks are a c.6 km thick group of volcaniclastic and bentonitic sediment horizons.

The Uriconian have long thought to be older than the Longmyndian and generally outcrop to the southeast of the latter within northeast-southwest trending lineaments suggesting basement influence for the regional structure.
On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.
The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.
It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.
Snag Rocks, ( ) also known as Rocas Bravo, is a cluster of rocks off the west coast of the Antarctic Peninsula.
This cluster of rocks lies mid-channel in the French Passage between the Roca Islands and the Myriad Islands, in the Wilhelm Archipelago.

Photographed from the helicopter of HMS Protector in March 1958, Snag Rocks was so named by United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) because the feature presents a hazard or obstacle to navigation.
Ramp Rocks ( ) are three barren rocks, the largest being 23 m high, lying 2.5 nautical miles (4.6 km) northwest of Johannesen Point, Main Island, at the west extremity of South Georgia.
The name "Laavebrua," a descriptive Norwegian term meaning literally "threshing floor bridge" or "barn bridge," was used for the largest rock by whalers and sealers at South Georgia.
In Norwegian barns used for storing hay, there is a ramp up which the wagons are driven before tipping.
"Laavebrua," which is not strictly translatable, is this ramp.
The United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) recommended in 1954 that "Ramp Rock" be approved for the large rock, but in 1976 altered the name to Ramp Rocks to include the three rocks.
The name Låvebrua Island is already approved for an island off Deception Island.
A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.
The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.
Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.
Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.
In January 1227, when the sea had frozen hard, a large Crusader army after the call of the Papal legate William of Modena was gathered.
According to the Chronicle of Livonia, 20 000 troops had set out to Muhu from the estuary of Pärnu River and moved along the ice.
Since the ice was said to have been very slippery, they reached the stronghold of Muhu on the ninth day.
After seeing such a large force, the people of Muhu offered truce and allowed themselves to be baptised.
Most of the Germans didn't accept it and so they started besieging the stronghold.
The first rush was fend off with rocks and spears.
The besiegers threw rocks into the stronghold with bricoles, built a turret and undermined the wall.

A drawing room play is a type of play, developed during the Victorian period in the United Kingdom, in which the actions take place in a drawing room or which is designed to be reenacted in the drawing room of a home.

The common practice of entertaining a guest in the home led to the creation of this category of plays and while the drawing room itself has fallen out of favour, the play format has continued to provide a source of entertainment.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?
hop1_docs: Eze-Igwe | The Oguta kingdom was established in like manners to the Benin Kingdom of Nigeria.  Many people in Oguta believe that the Oguta people migrated from the ancient Benin Kingdom.  They argue that the administrative set up, titles and common names have similarities to those of Bini people.  Although the people of Oguta do not call their king Oba as the Bini do, both kingdoms have some striking parallels.  The Binis have Iyase, in Oguta it is called Iyasara, there is Ogine among the Bini and Ogana in Oguta.  Palm oil and plantain are called ofigbo and ipa in both Edo language and Oguta dialect to this day.  Although the Oguta people speak a dialect of the Igbo language, some of their words are unmistakably of Bini origin.

Recreation room | A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.  The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.  Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.  Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.

Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song) | "Do What You Do" is a single by American R&B singer Jermaine Jackson, sibling of singers Michael and Janet Jackson and former member of The Jackson 5.  It was released as the second single his album, which was called "Jermaine Jackson" in the United States and "Dynamite" in the United Kingdom and other countries.
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do | "Hey, Hey, What Can I Do" is a song by the English rock group Led Zeppelin, released in 1970 as the B-side of "Immigrant Song" outside the United Kingdom.

On the Rocks (play) | On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.  The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.  It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.

That's What People Do | That's What People Do is an independently released album by the rock band Thousand Foot Krutch.  Rhyme Animal is the first and only single from this album, released in Canada.  The tracks titled "Rhyme Animal", "Small Town", "Lift It" and "Set It Off" were rerecorded in 2000 for the album Set It Off and "Rhyme Animal" was re-titled "Rhime Animal".  This is the only TFK album to have no music videos, until the 2009 record "Welcome to the Masquerade".  Trevor McNevan is the only member on the album who is still in the band.

Big Tent Revival | Big Tent Revival is a Christian rock band that formed in 1991, toured extensively, disbanded in 2000, and reformed in 2012.  They were featured at the Harvest Crusades.  Their most popular songs were "Two Sets of Jones'", "Choose Life", and "What Would Jesus Do?" .  The first told a story about two different couples in which one trusted in Jesus through the storms of life and the other didn't.  The second was used as an invitational at Harvest Crusade altar calls.  The last was part of the WWJD movement that encouraged people to consider what Jesus would do in real-life situations.
hop1_titles: Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song)
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage [5] states the entire play is set in the "Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street." Bridge entity: Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street. Missing fact: the passages do not say what people in the United Kingdom commonly call that location (the building's colloquial name). Retrieval directions: open the "10 Downing Street" (No. 10) article or search '"10 Downing Street" "commonly called" OR "also known as" site:wikipedia.org' and check the lead/aliases for the usual UK name (e.g., "Number 10"). Clarification: none needed unless you meant a different play or a different "10 Downing Street."
response.hop2_query: site:wikipedia.org "10 Downing Street" "commonly known as" OR "commonly called" OR "also known as" OR "Number 10" OR "No. 10"
hop2_docs: Pribnow box | The Pribnow box (also known as the Pribnow-Schaller box) is the sequence "TATAAT" of six nucleotides (thymine, adenine, thymine, etc.) that is an essential part of a promoter site on DNA for transcription to occur in bacteria.  It is an idealized or consensus sequence—that is, it shows the most frequently occurring base at each position in a large number of promoters analyzed; individual promoters often vary from the consensus at one or more positions.  It is also commonly called the -10 sequence, because it is centered roughly 10 base pairs upstream from the site of initiation of transcription.

10 Downing Street | 10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.

Geometric progression | In mathematics, a geometric progression, also known as a geometric sequence, is a sequence of numbers where each term after the first is found by multiplying the previous one by a fixed, non-zero number called the "common ratio".  For example, the sequence 2, 6, 18, 54, ... is a geometric progression with common ratio 3.  Similarly 10, 5, 2.5, 1.25, ... is a geometric sequence with common ratio 1/2.

List of residents of 10 Downing Street | Number 10 Downing Street is the residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.  The headquarters of Her Majesty's Government, it is situated on Downing Street in the City of Westminster in London, England.

Farthing Common | Farthing Common is an area of common land about 10 km northwest of Folkestone in East Kent.  It is one of the highest points of the North Downs at 185 metres above sea level, and was the junction of the ancient trackway along the Downs from Folkestone, with the Roman road (now the B2068 Stone Street) between Lympne and Canterbury.

1:10 radio-controlled off-road buggy | The 1:10 radio-controlled off-road buggy is a 1:10 scale radio-controlled dune buggy designed for off-road racing.  These cars are originally based on their full-scale equivalents that are commonly found in desert racing.  The buggies are split into two race categories, two (2WD) and four-wheel drive (4WD).  These can easily be distinguished visually by their wheel size at the front.  Cars are typically electric powered, but nitro versions do exist but are less common because racing classes exist for electric cars.  The class is inexpensive and similar to a number of other classes, and this makes them popular with newcomers.  The cars are also known as 1/10 off-road.

11 Downing Street | 11 Downing Street (sometimes referred to as just Number 11) is the official residence of Britain's Chancellor of the Exchequer (who traditionally also has the title of Second Lord of the Treasury).  The residence, in Downing Street in London, was built alongside the official residence of the Prime Minister at Number 10 in 1682.
hop2_titles: List of residents of 10 Downing Street
response.summary_2: People in the United Kingdom commonly call the location "Number 10" (No. 10). 10 Downing Street is colloquially known as "Number 10" and is the Prime Minister's official residence/headquarters, so the Cabinet Room at 10 Downing Street is commonly referred to as being at Number 10.
